questions.question: In the K. Umadevi v. Government of Tamil Nadu case, 2022, the Supreme Court granted maternity benefits for the third child under the Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act, 2017, stating it is a constitutional right. Which fundamental right did the Court link this benefit to?
questions.solution:
Maternity Benefits and Article 21

In the K. Umadevi case (2022), the Supreme Court emphasized that maternity benefits are not just statutory entitlements but are fundamental to a woman's Right to Life with dignity under Article 21. The Court held that denying such benefits, even for a third child, interferes with a woman's reproductive choices and her right to health.

questions.question: Which of the following options correctly describes the scope of judicial review under the Constitution of India?
questions.option_1: Both, Supreme Court and High Courts can strike down unconstitutional laws.
questions.option_2: Judicial review is explicitly mentioned in the Constitution (Article 13).
questions.option_3: Courts can review any law only after approval by the Parliament.
questions.option_4: Only the Supreme Court can declare laws unconstitutional.
questions.solution:
Scope of Judicial Review

In India, the power of judicial review is exercised by both the Supreme Court (Article 32) and the High Courts (Article 226). They have the authority to examine the constitutionality of legislative enactments and executive orders, and can strike them down if they violate the Constitution.

questions.question: Why does a diverse society need a constitution?
questions.option_1: To provide basic rules for coordination and assurance
questions.option_3: To allow rulers to dominate others
questions.solution:
Purpose of a Constitution

In a diverse society, a constitution serves as a set of basic rules that allow for minimal coordination among members of society. It provides the assurance that everyone will abide by these rules, defining how the government is constituted and what its limits are.

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right was removed from the Indian Constitution by the 44ᵗʰ Amendment Act, 1978?
questions.solution:
Removal of Right to Property

The 44th Amendment Act (1978) removed the Right to Property from the list of Fundamental Rights (formerly Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31). It was converted into a legal/constitutional right under Article 300A in Part XII of the Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following constitutional mechanisms ensures political representation for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in the Indian Parliament?
questions.solution:
Political Representation (SC/ST)

Article 330 provides for the reservation of seats for SCs and STs in the Lok Sabha, while Article 332 provides for similar reservations in the State Legislative Assemblies. This ensures that these communities have a guaranteed voice in the country's legislative process.

questions.question: Which part of the Indian Constitution deals with special provisions for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and other backwards classes?
questions.solution:
Part XVI: Special Provisions

Part XVI of the Indian Constitution (Articles 330 to 342A) deals with Special Provisions Relating to Certain Classes. This includes reservations in legislatures, the appointment of Special Officers, and the establishment of National Commissions for SCs, STs, and OBCs.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Right to Property in India is correct?
questions.option_1: It was completely abolished by the Forty-Second Amendment (1976).
questions.option_2: It is no longer a fundamental right.
questions.option_3: It is a fundamental right under Article 31 (now omitted).
questions.option_4: It is a fundamental right under Article 32 of the Constitution.
questions.solution:
Right to Property Today

The Right to Property is currently a Constitutional right, not a Fundamental Right. This means that while the state cannot take away a person's property without the 'authority of law', a citizen cannot move the Supreme Court directly under Article 32 for its enforcement as they would for a Fundamental Right.

questions.question: Which of the following is a feature of the State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) in India?
questions.option_1: It is a permanent body and cannot be dissolved
questions.option_2: It can be dissolved like the Legislative Assembly
questions.option_3: The people directly elect their members
questions.option_4: It has a fixed term of five years
questions.solution:
The Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad)

The State Legislative Council is a permanent body and is not subject to dissolution. While the body itself remains, one-third of its members retire every second year. Each member serves a six-year term, similar to the Rajya Sabha at the Union level.

questions.question: When was the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) established to investigate cases of human rights violations in India?
questions.solution:
Establishment of NHRC

The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) was established on 12 October 1993 under the Protection of Human Rights Ordinance, which was later replaced by the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. It serves as the 'watchdog of human rights' in the country.

questions.question: Who is responsible for the administration of Union Territories like Chandigarh and Lakshadweep?
questions.option_1: Elected Chief Ministers of the Union Territories
questions.solution:
Administration of Union Territories

Under Article 239, every Union Territory is administered by the President of India, acting through an administrator (designated as Lieutenant Governor or Administrator) appointed by him. Unlike states, UTs are under the direct control and supervision of the Central Government.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a Central Armed Police Force?
questions.solution:
Central Armed Police Forces (CAPF)

The CAPFs refer to seven security forces in India under the authority of the Ministry of Home Affairs, including the BSF, CRPF, and ITBP. The Criminal Investigation Department (CID) is not a CAPF; it is a specialized unit of the State Police forces.

questions.question: Which of the following subjects is addressed by the Tenth Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution:
Tenth Schedule: Anti-Defection

The Tenth Schedule was added by the 52nd Amendment in 1985 to curb the 'Aya Ram Gaya Ram' culture of political defections. It lays down the process by which legislators may be disqualified on grounds of defection from one political party to another.

questions.question: The Right to Protection in respect of conviction for offences (including protection from double jeopardy and self-incrimination) is covered under:
questions.solution:
Article 20: Protection in Conviction

Article 20 provides three vital protections to accused persons: Ex-post facto law (no person shall be convicted of an act that was not a crime at the time of its commission), Double Jeopardy (no person shall be punished for the same offence more than once), and Prohibition against Self-Incrimination.

questions.question: Article 6 deals with persons who migrated to India from _____.
questions.solution:
Article 6: Migration from Pakistan

Article 6 provides for the citizenship rights of certain persons who migrated to India from Pakistan before or after the commencement of the Constitution, provided they met specific residency and registration requirements.

questions.question: Which of the following States enjoys special provisions under Article 371A due to its unique customary law and land ownership practices?
questions.solution:
Special Provisions for Nagaland

Article 371A provides special status to Nagaland. It restricts the Parliament from legislating on Naga religious/social practices and land ownership without the state assembly's consent, acknowledging the unique cultural identity of the Naga people.

questions.question: Which of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution deals with Union Territories?
questions.solution:
Article 239: UT Provisions

Article 239 (under Part VIII) is the primary article dealing with the administration of Union Territories. It specifies that they are governed by the President through an Administrator. Subsequent articles like 239AA provide special provisions for Delhi.

questions.question: The RTI Act empowers citizens of India to seek information from which of the following?
questions.solution:
RTI and Public Authorities

The RTI Act primarily applies to public authorities, which include all government departments, ministries, and bodies substantially funded by the government. It generally does not cover private companies or foreign embassies unless they perform public functions.

questions.question: Which of the following is a mode of losing Indian citizenship?
questions.solution:
Loss of Citizenship

The Citizenship Act, 1955 prescribes three ways of losing citizenship: Renunciation (voluntary giving up), Termination (automatic loss upon acquiring another country's citizenship), and Deprivation (compulsory termination by the government for fraud/disloyalty).

questions.question: Lok Adalats in India have statutory status under which of the following enactments?
questions.solution:
Status of Lok Adalats

Lok Adalats (People's Courts) have been given statutory status under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987. Their awards are equivalent to a civil court decree and are non-appealable, helping to reduce the burden on regular courts.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution empowers the Parliament and State Legislatures to make laws for the whole or any part of the territory of India?
questions.solution:
Article 245: Legislative Jurisdiction

Article 245 defines the territorial extent of laws. It empowers Parliament to make laws for the whole or any part of India, and State Legislatures to make laws for the whole or any part of their respective State.

questions.question: Under what conditions can the Parliament legislate on matters in the State List according to constitutional provisions?
questions.option_1: When the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution.
questions.option_2: During a national emergency declared under Article 352.
questions.option_3: If a Governor requests it due to a financial emergency in the state.
questions.option_4: If the President signs a proclamation without Parliament's involvement.
questions.solution:
Parliament Legislating on State List

Under Article 249, if the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution supported by two-thirds of the members present and voting that it is necessary in the national interest, Parliament can make laws on a subject in the State List.

questions.question: Under which Article of the Indian Constitution is citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution defined?
questions.solution:
Article 5: Citizenship at Commencement

Article 5 defines citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution (January 26, 1950). It grants citizenship to persons who had their domicile in India and were born in India, or whose parents were born in India, or who were ordinarily resident for at least five years.

questions.question: Public Interest Litigation (PIL) in India is primarily associated with which of the following?
questions.solution:
PIL and Judicial Activism

Public Interest Litigation (PIL) is a tool of judicial activism where the court entertains litigation filed by any public-spirited individual for the protection of 'public interest' (e.g., environment, human rights), even if the filer is not personally affected.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of Union Territories were merged to form a single Indian Union Territory in 2020?
questions.option_4: Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu
questions.solution:
Merge of UTs (2020)

On 26 January 2020, the Union Territories of Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu were merged into a single Union Territory to improve administrative efficiency and reduce the cost of governance.

questions.question: The Right to Constitutional Remedies, allowing individuals to move the Supreme Court directly for the enforcement of rights, is given under:
questions.solution:
Article 32: Heart and Soul

Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) allows citizens to approach the Supreme Court directly for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar famously called this article the 'Heart and Soul of the Constitution'.
